summary: Lex Fridman interviews Commander David Fravor about his career as a Navy pilot, his experience witnessing and chasing the 'Tic Tac' UFO during the USS Nimitz incident in 2004, and broader discussions about UFOs/UAPs, propulsion technology, and the nature of reality. They also discuss the challenges of balancing national security with scientific inquiry and the potential for private sector innovation in aerospace.

main_entities.name: Tic Tac
main_entities.context: Unidentified Flying Object witnessed by David Fravor and others in 2004, described as a white, wingless, 40-foot long object.

main_entities.name: USS Nimitz UFO incident
main_entities.context: The series of UFO sightings and encounters involving the USS Nimitz carrier strike group in 2004, including the Tic Tac incident.

main_entities.name: Top Gun
main_entities.context: The Navy Fighter Weapons School, where David Fravor trained. Discussion of its realism compared to the movie.

main_entities.name: SR-71 Blackbird
main_entities.context: High-speed reconnaissance aircraft, considered by Fravor to be an engineering marvel.

main_entities.name: F/A-18F Super Hornet
main_entities.context: Fighter jet flown by David Fravor, used in the Tic Tac incident.

main_entities.name: Project Blue Book
main_entities.context: Former US Air Force project to study unidentified flying objects.

main_entities.name: ATIP (Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program)
main_entities.context: Pentagon program that investigated UFOs/UAPs, run by Lou Elizondo.

main_entities.name: Aegis Cruiser
main_entities.context: USS Princeton, a cruiser equipped with advanced radar systems that initially detected the Tic Tac object.

main_entities.name: Skunk Works
main_entities.context: Lockheed Martin's advanced projects division, known for developing innovative aircraft.

main_entities.name: SpaceX
main_entities.context: Private space exploration company founded by Elon Musk, discussed for its innovation and cost-effectiveness.

main_entities.name: Elon Musk
main_entities.context: Founder of SpaceX, discussed for his contributions to space exploration and innovative approach.

main_entities.name: FLIR (Forward Looking Infrared)
main_entities.context: Infrared camera system used to record the Tic Tac video.

main_entities.name: Gimbal video
main_entities.context: One of the three videos released by the Pentagon, showing a rotating object.

main_entities.name: Go Fast video
main_entities.context: One of the three videos released by the Pentagon, showing an object near the surface of the ocean.

key_details.fact: The Tic Tac object moved in ways that defied conventional aircraft capabilities, with abrupt changes in direction and rapid acceleration.
The USS Princeton tracked the objects for about two weeks before the encounter with Fravor and his team.
The original FLIR video of the Tic Tac is about 1 minute 30 seconds long, contrary to some rumors of a longer version.
Active jamming of the radar system was detected when trying to lock onto the Tic Tac, which is technically an act of war.
David Fravor attributes his success to never taking 'no' for an answer and believing in his own capabilities.
The hierarchy at Top Gun is based on seniority at the school, not necessarily rank.
The SR-71 still holds the speed record for any plane.
Fravor got a Super Hornet up to Mach 1.78 off the coast of California.
The official investigation of the Tic Tac incident was never officially released due to FOIA exemptions.
